author: A Treatise on Grace and Free Will
book: Two Letters Written by Augustin to Valentinus and the Monks of Adrumetum.

classification.cautionReason: No major mainstream heresy warning is attached by the preview classifier.

lineage: A Treatise on Grace and Free Will.
Two Letters Written by Augustin to Valentinus and the Monks of Adrumetum.

metadata.author: A Treatise on Grace and Free Will

title: Letter II

verses.book: Two Letters Written by Augustin to Valentinus and the Monks of Adrumetum.
verses.text: To my very dear lord and most honoured brother among the members of Christ, Valentinus, and to the brethren that are with you, Augustin sends greeting in the Lord.

verses.book: Two Letters Written by Augustin to Valentinus and the Monks of Adrumetum.
verses.text: That Cresconius and Felix, and another Felix, the servants of God, who came to us from your brotherhood, have spent Easter with us is known to your Love.

verses.book: Two Letters Written by Augustin to Valentinus and the Monks of Adrumetum.
verses.text: We have detained them somewhile longer in order that they might return to you better instructed against the new Pelagian heretics, into whose error every one falls who supposes that it is according to any human merits that the grace of God is given to us, which alone delivers a man through Jesus Christ our Lord.

verses.book: Two Letters Written by Augustin to Valentinus and the Monks of Adrumetum.
verses.text: But he, too, is no less in error who thinks that, when the Lord shall come to judgment, a man is not judged according to his works who has been able to use throughout his life free choice of will.

verses.book: Two Letters Written by Augustin to Valentinus and the Monks of Adrumetum.
verses.text: For only infants, who have not yet done any works of their own, either good or bad, will be condemned on account of original sin alone, when they have not been delivered by the Saviour’s grace in the laver of regeneration.

verses.book: Two Letters Written by Augustin to Valentinus and the Monks of Adrumetum.
verses.text: As for all others who, in the use of their free will, have added to original sin, sins of their own commission, but who have not been delivered by God’s grace from the power of darkness and removed into the kingdom of Christ, they will receive judgment according to the deserts not of their original sin only, but also of the acts of their own will.

verses.book: Two Letters Written by Augustin to Valentinus and the Monks of Adrumetum.
verses.text: The good, indeed, shall receive their reward according to the merits of their own good-will, but then they received this very good-will through the grace of God; and thus is accomplished that sentence of Scripture, “Indignation and wrath, tribulation and anguish, upon every soul of man that doeth evil, of the Jew first, and also of the Gentile: but glory, honour, and peace to every man that worketh good; to the Jew first, and also to the Gentile.”

verses.book: Two Letters Written by Augustin to Valentinus and the Monks of Adrumetum.
verses.text: Touching the very difficult question of will and grace, I have felt no need of treating it further in this letter, having given them another letter also when they were about to return in greater haste.

verses.book: Two Letters Written by Augustin to Valentinus and the Monks of Adrumetum.
verses.text: I have written a book likewise for you, and if you, by the Lord’s help, read it, and have a lively understanding of it, I think that no further dissension on this subject will arise among you.

verses.book: Two Letters Written by Augustin to Valentinus and the Monks of Adrumetum.
verses.text: They take with them other documents besides, which, as we supposed, ought to be sent to you, in order that from these you may ascertain what means the catholic Church has adopted for repelling, in God’s mercy, the poison of the Pelagian heresy.

verses.book: Two Letters Written by Augustin to Valentinus and the Monks of Adrumetum.
verses.text: For the letters to Pope Innocent, Bishop of Rome, from the Council of the province of Carthage, and from the Council of Numidia, and one written with exceeding care by five bishops, and what he wrote back to these three; our letter also to Pope Zosimus about the African Council, and his answer addressed to all bishops throughout the world; and a brief constitution, which we drew up against the error itself at a later plenary Council of all Africa; and the above-mentioned book of mine, which I have just written for you,—all these we have both read over with them, while they were with us, and have now despatched by their hands to you.

verses.book: Two Letters Written by Augustin to Valentinus and the Monks of Adrumetum.
verses.text: Furthermore, we have read to them the work of the most blessed martyr Cyprian on the Lord’s Prayer, and have pointed out to them how He taught that all things pertaining to our morals, which constitute right living, must be sought from our Father which is in heaven, lest, by presuming on free will, we fall from divine grace.

verses.book: Two Letters Written by Augustin to Valentinus and the Monks of Adrumetum.
verses.text: From the same treatise we have also shown them how the same glorious martyr has taught us that it behoves us to pray even for our enemies who have not yet believed in Christ, that they may believe; which would of course be all in vain unless the Church believed that even the evil and unbelieving wills of men might, by the grace of God, be converted to good.

verses.book: Two Letters Written by Augustin to Valentinus and the Monks of Adrumetum.
verses.text: This book of St. Cyprian, however, we have not sent you, because they told us that you possessed it among yourselves already.

verses.book: Two Letters Written by Augustin to Valentinus and the Monks of Adrumetum.
verses.text: My letter, also, which had been sent to Sixtus, presbyter of the Church at Rome and which they brought with them to us, we read over with them, and pointed out how that it had been written in opposition to those who say that God’s grace is bestowed according to our merits,—that is to say, in opposition to the same Pelagians.

verses.book: Two Letters Written by Augustin to Valentinus and the Monks of Adrumetum.
verses.text: As far, then, as lay in our power, we have used our influence with them, as both your brethren and our own, with a view to their persevering in the soundness of the catholic faith, which neither denies free will whether for an evil or a good life, nor attributes to it so much power that it can avail anything without God’s grace, whether that it may be changed from evil to good, or that it may persevere in the pursuit of good, or that it may attain to eternal good when there is no further fear of failure.

verses.book: Two Letters Written by Augustin to Valentinus and the Monks of Adrumetum.
verses.text: To yourselves, too, my most dearly beloved, I also, in this letter, give the same exhortation which the apostle addresses to us all, “not to think of yourselves more highly than you ought to think; but to think soberly, according as God hath dealt to every man the measure of faith.”

verses.book: Two Letters Written by Augustin to Valentinus and the Monks of Adrumetum.
verses.text: Mark well the counsel which the Holy Ghost gives us by Solomon: “Make straight paths for thy feet, and order thy ways aright.

verses.book: Two Letters Written by Augustin to Valentinus and the Monks of Adrumetum.
verses.text: Turn not aside to the right hand nor to the left, but turn away thy foot from the evil way; for the Lord knoweth the ways on the right hand, but those on the left are perverse.

verses.book: Two Letters Written by Augustin to Valentinus and the Monks of Adrumetum.
verses.text: He will make thy ways straight, and will direct thy steps in peace.”

verses.book: Two Letters Written by Augustin to Valentinus and the Monks of Adrumetum.
verses.text: Now consider, my brethren, that in these words of Holy Scripture, if there were no free will, it would not be said, “Make straight paths for thy feet, and order thy ways; turn not aside to the right hand, nor to the left.”

verses.book: Two Letters Written by Augustin to Valentinus and the Monks of Adrumetum.
verses.text: Nor yet, were this possible for us to achieve without the grace of God, would it be afterwards added, “He will make thy ways straight, and will direct thy steps in peace.”

verses.book: Two Letters Written by Augustin to Valentinus and the Monks of Adrumetum.
verses.text: Decline, therefore, neither to the right hand nor to the left, although the paths on the right hand are praised, and those on the left hand are blamed.

verses.book: Two Letters Written by Augustin to Valentinus and the Monks of Adrumetum.
verses.text: This is why he added, “Turn away thy foot from the evil way,”—that is, from the left-hand path.

verses.book: Two Letters Written by Augustin to Valentinus and the Monks of Adrumetum.
verses.text: This he makes manifest in the following words, saying, “For the Lord knoweth the ways on the right hand; but those on the left are perverse.”

verses.book: Two Letters Written by Augustin to Valentinus and the Monks of Adrumetum.
verses.text: In those ways we ought surely to walk which the Lord knows; and it is of these that we read in the Psalm, “The Lord knoweth the way of the righteous, but the way of the ungodly shall perish;” for this way, which is on the left hand, the Lord does not know.

verses.book: Two Letters Written by Augustin to Valentinus and the Monks of Adrumetum.
verses.text: As He will also say at last to such as are placed on His left hand at the day of judgment: “I know you not.”

verses.book: Two Letters Written by Augustin to Valentinus and the Monks of Adrumetum.
verses.text: Now what is that which He knows not, who knows all things, both good and evil, in man?

verses.book: Two Letters Written by Augustin to Valentinus and the Monks of Adrumetum.
verses.text: But what is the meaning of the words, “I know you not,” unless it be that you are now such as I never made you?

verses.book: Two Letters Written by Augustin to Valentinus and the Monks of Adrumetum.
verses.text: Precisely as that passage runs, which is spoken of the Lord Jesus Christ, that “He knew no sin.”

verses.book: Two Letters Written by Augustin to Valentinus and the Monks of Adrumetum.
verses.text: How knew it not, except that He had never made it?

verses.book: Two Letters Written by Augustin to Valentinus and the Monks of Adrumetum.
verses.text: And, therefore, how is to be understood the passage, “The ways which are on the right hand the Lord knoweth,” except in the sense that He made those ways Himself,—even “the paths of the righteous,” which no doubt are “those good works that God,” as the apostle tells us, “hath before ordained that we should walk in them”?

verses.book: Two Letters Written by Augustin to Valentinus and the Monks of Adrumetum.
verses.text: Whereas the left-hand ways—those perverse paths of the unrighteous—He truly knows nothing of, because He never made them for man, but man made them for himself.

verses.book: Two Letters Written by Augustin to Valentinus and the Monks of Adrumetum.
verses.text: Wherefore He says, “The perverse ways of the wicked I utterly abhor; they are on the left hand.”

verses.book: Two Letters Written by Augustin to Valentinus and the Monks of Adrumetum.
verses.text: But the reply is made: Why did He say, “Turn not aside to the right hand, nor to the left,” when he clearly ought rather to have said, Keep to the right hand, and turn not off to the left, if the right-hand paths are good?

verses.book: Two Letters Written by Augustin to Valentinus and the Monks of Adrumetum.
verses.text: Why, do we think, except this, that the paths on the right hand are so good that it is not good to turn off from them, even to the right?

verses.book: Two Letters Written by Augustin to Valentinus and the Monks of Adrumetum.
verses.text: For that man, indeed, is to be understood as declining to the right who chooses to attribute to himself, and not to God, even those good works which appertain to right-hand ways.

verses.book: Two Letters Written by Augustin to Valentinus and the Monks of Adrumetum.
verses.text: Hence it was that after saying, “For the Lord knoweth the ways on the right hand, but those on the left hand are perverse,” as if the objection were raised to Him, Wherefore, then, do you not wish us to turn aside to the right?

verses.book: Two Letters Written by Augustin to Valentinus and the Monks of Adrumetum.
verses.text: He immediately added as follows: “He will Himself make thy paths straight, and will direct thy ways in peace.”

verses.book: Two Letters Written by Augustin to Valentinus and the Monks of Adrumetum.
verses.text: Understand, therefore, the precept, “Make straight paths for thy feet, and order thy ways aright,” in such a sense as to know that whenever you do all this, it is the Lord God who enables you to do it.

verses.book: Two Letters Written by Augustin to Valentinus and the Monks of Adrumetum.
verses.text: Then you will not turn off to the right, although you are walking in right-hand paths, not trusting in your own strength; and He will Himself be your strength, who will make straight paths for your feet, and will direct your ways in peace.

verses.book: Two Letters Written by Augustin to Valentinus and the Monks of Adrumetum.
verses.text: Wherefore, most dearly beloved, whosoever says, My will suffices for me to perform good works, declines to the right.

verses.book: Two Letters Written by Augustin to Valentinus and the Monks of Adrumetum.
verses.text: But, on the other hand, they who think that a good way of life should be forsaken, when they hear God’s grace so preached as to lead to the supposition and belief that it of itself makes men’s wills from evil to good, and it even of itself keeps them what it has made them; and who, as the result of this opinion, go on to say, “Let us do evil that good may come,”—these persons decline to the left.

verses.book: Two Letters Written by Augustin to Valentinus and the Monks of Adrumetum.
verses.text: This is the reason why he said to you, “Turn not aside to the right hand, nor to the left;” in other words, do not uphold free will in such wise as to attribute good works to it without the grace of God, nor so defend and maintain grace as if, by reason of it, you may love evil works in security and safety,—which may God’s grace itself avert from you!

verses.book: Two Letters Written by Augustin to Valentinus and the Monks of Adrumetum.
verses.text: Now it was the words of such as these which the apostle had in view when he said, “What shall we say, then?

verses.book: Two Letters Written by Augustin to Valentinus and the Monks of Adrumetum.
verses.text: Shall we continue in sin that grace may abound?”

verses.book: Two Letters Written by Augustin to Valentinus and the Monks of Adrumetum.
verses.text: And to this cavil of erring men, who know nothing about the grace of God, he returned such an answer as he ought in these words: “God forbid.

verses.book: Two Letters Written by Augustin to Valentinus and the Monks of Adrumetum.
verses.text: How shall we, that are dead to sin, live any longer therein?”

verses.book: Two Letters Written by Augustin to Valentinus and the Monks of Adrumetum.
verses.text: Nothing could have been said more succinctly, and yet to the point.

verses.book: Two Letters Written by Augustin to Valentinus and the Monks of Adrumetum.
verses.text: For what more useful gift does the grace of God confer upon us, in this present evil world, than our dying unto sin?

verses.book: Two Letters Written by Augustin to Valentinus and the Monks of Adrumetum.
verses.text: Hence he shows himself ungrateful to grace itself who chooses to live in sin by reason of that whereby we die unto sin.

verses.book: Two Letters Written by Augustin to Valentinus and the Monks of Adrumetum.
verses.text: May God, however, who is rich in mercy, grant you both to think soundly and wisely, and to continue perseveringly and progressively to the end in every good determination and purpose.

verses.book: Two Letters Written by Augustin to Valentinus and the Monks of Adrumetum.
verses.text: For yourselves, for us, for all who love you, and for those who hate you, pray that this gift may be attained,—pray earnestly and vigilantly in brotherly peace.

verses.book: Two Letters Written by Augustin to Valentinus and the Monks of Adrumetum.
verses.text: Live unto God.

verses.book: Two Letters Written by Augustin to Valentinus and the Monks of Adrumetum.
verses.text: If I deserve any favour at your hands, let brother Florus come to me.
